query: ECG changes suggestive of myocardial ischemia in smokers

retrieved_docs.title: The de Winter ECG Pattern -- Epidemiology
retrieved_docs.content: The de Winter electrocardiogram pattern is an infrequent presentation, reported to occur in 2% to 3.4% of patients with anterior myocardial infarction. [8] [10] [14] [15] Because the de Winter electrocardiogram pattern relates to patients presenting with ACS, the risk factors associated with this pattern are similar to those of ischemic heart disease (eg, increased age, smoking, dyslipidemia, hypertension, diabetes mellitus, and obesity). [1] [2] [3] [14] In some studies, male patients were more likely to present with this pattern, and some authors have reported that individuals with this pattern tend to be younger than those who present with an anterior STEMI. [14] [15] [10] [15]

retrieved_docs.content: The American Heart Association recommends combining behavioral and pharmacologic approaches to maximize quit rates. [41]

retrieved_docs.title: Ambulatory ECG Monitoring -- Indications -- Chest Pain and Ischemic Episodes
retrieved_docs.content: Ambulatory ECG monitoring is valuable for patients with atypical chest pain associated with stresses other than exercise by enabling the assessment of myocardial ischemia during routine activities. Ambulatory ECG monitoring sometimes unveils an underlying arrhythmia as the initiator of chest pain. In a study by Stern et al of 50 patients with precordial pain, where positive results in ambulatory ECG monitoring were characterized by ST-segment deviations of ≥1 mm from the baseline pattern or significant T wave inversions demonstrated that of the 32 patients with positive abnormalities, 28 had severe coronary disease during coronary angiography. Conversely, among the 18 patients without positive monitoring results, only 3 met the angiographic criteria for severe coronary disease. [35]

retrieved_docs.content: Behavioral interventions include motivational interviewing (Ask, Advise, Assess, Assist, Arrange for follow-up).

retrieved_docs.title: Risk Factors for Coronary Artery Disease -- Clinical Significance -- Smoking
retrieved_docs.content: Varenicline doubled the chances of smoking cessation. [109] There have been rare reports of neuropsychiatric adverse effects with varenicline. The FDA removed this black box warning in 2016 after noting that the risk was lower than expected.

retrieved_docs.title: Acute Myocardial Infarction -- Evaluation
retrieved_docs.content: Patients presenting with symptoms of MI may not exhibit diagnostic ST-elevation ECG abnormalities. In cases in which patients have typical chest pain but no obvious ST elevation, further investigation for NSTEMI is warranted. Subtle abnormalities on ECG, such as ST depressions and T-wave changes, may be present. Serial ECGs can be helpful in looking for dynamic changes. ECG findings without acute changes or any abnormalities are common in NSTEMI.

retrieved_docs.content: Pharmacologic interventions such as nicotine replacement therapy, varenicline, and bupropion reduce cravings and withdrawal symptoms.

retrieved_docs.title: Unstable Angina -- Continuing Education Activity
retrieved_docs.content: Unstable angina is a clinical syndrome characterized by myocardial ischemia without sustained ST-segment elevation or biochemical evidence of myocardial necrosis. This condition falls within the spectrum of acute coronary syndromes, along with ST-elevation myocardial infarction and non–ST-elevation myocardial infarction. Unstable angina typically presents as chest pain that is new in onset, occurs at rest, or exhibits a worsening pattern. The condition is associated with an increased risk of myocardial infarction and requires prompt recognition and management. Diagnosis is based on clinical assessment, electrocardiographic changes, and normal or minimally elevated cardiac biomarkers. Risk factors include advanced age, smoking, hypertension, diabetes mellitus, hyperlipidemia, and a history of coronary artery disease. Management involves initiation of antiplatelet therapy, anticoagulation, nitrates, β-blockers, and risk stratification to determine the need for early invasive intervention.

retrieved_docs.title: Risk Factors for Coronary Artery Disease -- Clinical Significance -- Smoking
retrieved_docs.content: The risk of CAD drops to a level of lifetime nonsmokers within 4 years of quitting, according to the FDA, and within 10 years, according to the CDC. [106] [107]

retrieved_docs.title: Wellens Syndrome -- Epidemiology
retrieved_docs.content: Wellens syndrome represents a pre-infarction state of coronary artery disease. The risk factors for Wellens syndrome are therefore the same as those for coronary artery disease including dyslipidemia, hypertension, diabetes, sedentary lifestyle, obesity, familial history, and smoking. The ECG pattern of Wellens syndrome is relatively common in patients who exhibit symptoms consistent with unstable angina. In studies performed by Dr. Wellens and colleagues, the ECG pattern was present in 14% to 18% of patients admitted for unstable angina.

retrieved_docs.title: Posterior Myocardial Infarction -- Etiology
retrieved_docs.content: There are a number of risk factors for coronary artery disease and, by extension, myocardial infarction. The HEART score is a diagnostic pathway used in patients older than 21 years of age who present with symptoms suggestive of ACS. It is used to stratify risk and is predictive of the 6-week risk of a major adverse cardiac event. It is not for use in patients with ST-elevation or other new EKG changes, hypotension, or other comorbid conditions requiring admission, but the risk factors apply to any patient in whom there is a concern for acute coronary syndrome (ACS). Risk factors considered as a component of the HEART score include hypertension, hypercholesterolemia, diabetes mellitus, obesity (BMI over 30 kg/m^2), smoking (current or smoking cessation three months), positive family history (parent or sibling with coronary vascular disease [CVD] before age 65); atherosclerotic disease: prior MI, percutaneous coronary intervention (PCI), coronary artery bypass grafting (CABG), cerebrovascular accident (CVA), transient ischemic attack (TIA), or peripheral arterial disease. [1]

retrieved_docs.title: Ambulatory ECG Monitoring -- Indications -- Chest Pain and Ischemic Episodes
retrieved_docs.content: Standard 24-hour ambulatory ECG monitoring helps recognize and evaluate early ischemic heart disease by recording dynamic changes, even when the resting 12-lead ECG appears normal. However, dynamic ST-segment changes may be confounded by body position or cardiac medications; cautious interpretation is warranted. [36] Crawford et al. In a study of 70 patients with chest pain, normal resting ECGs, and known significant coronary artery disease, Crawford et al determined a 62% sensitivity and 61% specificity rate for ST depression during ambulatory monitoring, concluding that continuous ambulatory ECG monitoring is limited in detecting or ruling out coronary artery disease in symptomatic patients with normal resting ECGs. [37]

retrieved_docs.title: Non–ST-Segment Elevation Myocardial Infarction -- Differential Diagnosis
retrieved_docs.content: Clinical conditions that can present with chest pain and have nonspecific ECG changes with an elevated troponin marker include: Myocarditis Pericarditis Pulmonary embolism Left ventricular aneurysm Prinzmetal angina Anxiety Disorder Aortic Stenosis Hypertensive emergency

retrieved_docs.title: The de Winter ECG Pattern -- Introduction
retrieved_docs.content: The de Winter electrocardiogram pattern is a finding that, in a proper setting, is highly suggestive of acute occlusion of the left anterior descending artery. This electrocardiogram finding consists of an ST-segment upsloping depression at the J point of 1 to 3 mm in leads V1 to V6, associated with tall, peaked positive T waves. The pattern is considered to be equivalent to an ST elevation as a clinical indication of myocardial infarction. Therefore, clinical recognition of this finding and the additional evaluation it should precipitate is critical to improving patient outcomes. Emergent consultation with a cardiologist should be undertaken when the de Winter electrocardiogram pattern is observed to allow for timely cardiac catheterization and primary percutaneous coronary intervention, if appropriate, to ensure appropriate reperfusion as per ST-elevation myocardial infarction (STEMI) guidelines. [2] [5] [7] [4]

retrieved_docs.title: Non–ST-Segment Elevation Myocardial Infarction -- Evaluation
retrieved_docs.content: History, ECG, and cardiac biomarkers are the mainstays in the evaluation. An ECG should be performed as soon as possible in patients presenting with chest pain or those with a concern for ACS. A normal ECG does not exclude ACS and NSTEMI. ST-elevation or anterior ST depression should be considered a STEMI until proven otherwise and treated as such. Findings suggestive of NSTEMI include transient ST elevation, ST depression, or new T wave inversions. ECG should be repeated at predetermined intervals or if symptoms return.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 12 -- Level 3 -- Community 2279
retrieved_docs.content: Acute Q wave myocardial infarction, acute ST segment elevation myocardial infarction (disorder), acute ST segment elevation myocardial infarction due to distal left anterior descending coronary artery occlusion, acute ST segment elevation myocardial infarction due to left coronary artery occlusion, acute ST segment elevation myocardial infarction due to mid left anterior descending coronary artery occlusion, acute ST segment elevation myocardial infarction due to occlusion of circumflex branch of left coronary artery, acute ST segment elevation myocardial infarction due to occlusion of distal portion of right coronary artery, acute ST segment elevation myocardial infarction due to occlusion of mid portion of right coronary artery, acute ST segment elevation myocardial infarction due to proximal left anterior descending coronary artery occlusion, acute ST segment elevation myocardial infarction due to right coronary artery occlusion, acute ST segment elevation myocardial infarction involving left anterior descending coronary artery, acute myocardial infarction due to occlusion of circumflex branch of left coronary artery, acute non‑Q wave infarction, acute non‑Q wave infarction – widespread, and acute non‑ST segment elevation myocardial infarction (disorder) all share the myocardium as their primary site of pathology. retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 3 -- Community 1831
retrieved_docs.content: Coronary artery disease (تصلب الشريان التاجي) is clinically associated with heart murmurs (نفخة قلبية). The presence of a heart murmur can serve as an important clinical indicator of underlying coronary artery pathology, reflecting turbulent blood flow that may result from atherosclerotic narrowing or ischemic changes in the coronary vessels. Recognizing this association helps clinicians consider coronary artery disease in the differential diagnosis when a murmur is detected, prompting further cardiovascular evaluation and appropriate management to reduce the risk of adverse cardiac events.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 19 -- Level 3 -- Community 2559
retrieved_docs.content: Acute Q wave infarction – widespread, acute ST‑segment elevation myocardial infarctions caused by occlusion of the distal left anterior descending coronary artery, the circumflex branch of the left coronary artery, the distal portion of the right coronary artery, the intermediate artery, the marginal branch of the right coronary artery, or the posterior descending branch of the right coronary artery, as well as acute ST‑segment elevation myocardial infarction involving the left anterior descending coronary artery and acute myocardial infarction due to left coronary artery occlusion, all represent distinct presentations of an acute myocardial infarction that share a common pathological substrate in the myocardium. Acute non‑Q wave infarction, acute non‑Q wave infarction – widespread, acute non‑ST‑segment elevation myocardial infarction (disorder), acute widespread myocardial infarction, postoperative acute myocardial infarction, and subsequent anterior non‑ST‑segment elevation myocardial infarction are additional variants of acute myocardial infarction, each reflecting different electrocardiographic patterns, timing, or clinical contexts, yet each also localizes its injury to the myocardial tissue. Collectively, these entities illustrate how diverse coronary artery occlusions, ECG morphologies, and clinical settings converge on myocardial damage as the central finding site, underscoring the importance of recognizing the specific type of infarction for appropriate diagnosis, risk stratification, and management in patient care.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 15 -- Level 3 -- Community 2197
retrieved_docs.content: Acute infarct is a cardiac pathology that can manifest with a wide range of morphologic patterns. It is associated with acute Q wave infarction – including the widespread form – and with acute Q wave myocardial infarction. Various forms of acute ST segment elevation myocardial infarction are also linked to acute infarct, such as the general disorder and specific presentations caused by occlusion of distinct coronary segments: the anterior descending branch of the left coronary artery, the distal portion of the right coronary artery, the mid portion of the right coronary artery, the posterior descending branch of the right coronary artery, and right coronary artery occlusion overall. In addition, acute infarct encompasses acute myocardial infarction due to occlusion of the circumflex branch of the left coronary artery, acute myocardial infarction due to right coronary artery occlusion, and acute myocardial infarction that occurs during a medical procedure. Non‑Q wave morphologies are included as well, with both acute non‑Q wave infarction and its widespread variant being associated with acute infarct. Furthermore, acute infarct is linked to acute non‑ST segment elevation myocardial infarction and to subsequent anterior non‑ST segment elevation myocardial infarction, reflecting its relevance to both initial and later ischemic events. Together, these associations illustrate that acute infarct can present with diverse electrocardiographic and anatomic features, emphasizing the importance of recognizing its various morphologies for accurate diagnosis and management.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 20 -- Level 3 -- Community 2526
retrieved_docs.content: Acute infarct is linked to a broad spectrum of myocardial infarction morphologies, encompassing both Q‑wave and non‑Q‑wave patterns as well as numerous ST‑segment elevation presentations that correspond to specific coronary artery occlusions. The entities associated with acute infarct include Acute Q wave infarction – widespread and Acute Q wave myocardial infarction, as well as Acute non‑Q wave infarction and Acute non‑Q wave infarction – widespread. Acute infarct also has associated morphologies of Acute ST segment elevation myocardial infarction due to occlusion of distinct arterial segments: distal left anterior descending coronary artery, left coronary artery, mid left anterior descending coronary artery, distal portion of the right coronary artery, intermediate artery, marginal branch of the right coronary artery, proximal portion of the right coronary artery, and right coronary artery overall. Additional specific forms tied to acute infarct are Acute myocardial infarction due to left coronary artery occlusion, Acute myocardial infarction due to occlusion of the circumflex branch of the left coronary artery, and the later‑occurring Subsequent anterior non‑ST segment elevation myocardial infarction. Together, these relationships illustrate that acute infarct serves as an overarching category that integrates diverse electrocardiographic and anatomic presentations of acute myocardial injury, aiding clinicians in recognizing the varied patterns of coronary artery involvement and guiding appropriate diagnostic and therapeutic strategies.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 21 -- Level 2 -- Community 918
retrieved_docs.content: Acute infarct is a broad diagnostic category that encompasses a variety of specific electro‑cardiographic and anatomic morphologies. Among the morphologies linked to acute infarct are Acute Q wave infarction – widespread and Acute non‑Q wave infarction (both focal and widespread), reflecting the presence or absence of Q‑wave development on the ECG. Acute infarct also includes numerous forms of Acute ST‑segment elevation myocardial infarction, each defined by the coronary artery segment that is occluded: the mid left anterior descending coronary artery, the proximal left anterior descending coronary artery, the anterior descending branch of the left coronary artery, the circumflex branch of the left coronary artery, the left coronary artery overall, the right coronary artery (including its distal portion and marginal branch), and the intermediate artery. In addition, Acute infarct is associated with Acute myocardial infarction due to left coronary artery occlusion and Acute myocardial infarction due to occlusion of the circumflex branch of the left coronary artery. A related but distinct pattern, Subsequent anterior non‑ST segment elevation myocardial infarction, is also listed as an associated morphology, indicating that non‑ST‑segment elevation events can follow an initial acute infarct. Together, these entities illustrate how acute infarct can present with diverse ECG patterns and coronary artery involvement, guiding clinicians in precise localization, risk stratification, and targeted therapeutic management.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 27 -- Level 2 -- Community 1103
retrieved_docs.content: Acute myocardial infarction encompasses a spectrum of disorders that all involve injury to the myocardium. Specific forms include Acute Q‑wave infarction – widespread, Acute ST‑segment elevation myocardial infarction (STEMI), and Acute STEMI due to occlusion of distinct coronary branches such as the left coronary artery, the left anterior descending (LAD) artery, the circumflex branch of the left coronary artery, the diagonal branch of the LAD, the intermediate artery, the marginal branch of the right coronary artery, the posterior descending branch of the right coronary artery, and the septal branch of the LAD. Additional variants are Acute myocardial infarction due to left coronary artery occlusion, Acute myocardial infarction due to circumflex branch occlusion, Acute myocardial infarction due to right coronary artery occlusion, Acute myocardial infarction occurring during a procedure, and Acute widespread myocardial infarction. Despite the differing anatomic sites of arterial blockage and electrocardiographic patterns (Q‑wave versus ST‑segment elevation), each of these entities shares the common finding site of the myocardium, underscoring that the primary pathological substrate in all these conditions is myocardial tissue damage. Recognizing the specific coronary artery involved helps guide reperfusion strategies and risk stratification, while the universal involvement of the myocardium highlights the need for prompt diagnosis and treatment to preserve cardiac function.
